reasoning: I will provide a concise summary of the article, highlighting the main event (a surge of automated comments to the FCC), the nature of the comments (similar wording, potential use of stolen data), the expert reactions (viewing this as a form of protest), related context (DDoS attack, John Oliver involvement), and the FCC's lack of comment. I’ll avoid detailed step-by-step reasoning and present a clear, compact recap.
output: Over the weekend, researchers detected a flood of automated comments to the FCC about net neutrality—more than 400,000 submissions with remarkably similar wording. Pro-net neutrality advocates argue for equal treatment of all internet traffic, while experts describe the activity as a new form of online protest using bots. Some comments appear to reuse personal data from breached sites to seem authentic, suggesting data from stolen databases may have been used. Observers from MIT and Harvard have noted the surge in automated activity. This followed a prior DDoS attack on the FCC’s comments system, and intense publicity around the issue (including John Oliver’s TV appearance). Critics worry that the bot-driven submissions could muddy public opinion and mislead the FCC into thinking there is less genuine opposition. The FCC has not yet commented on the matter.
